titleStmt.title: Letter from John Redmond to Major General Lovick Bransby Friend, 9 March 1916
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: John Redmond

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
Private. House of Commons March 9th, 1916. Dear General Friend,— I am much obliged to you for your letter
of the 8th March about the Irish Volunteer Training Corps. I
am glad to find that you still are of my view that it is not
too late, even now, to extend the usefulness of the Volunteers
in Ireland for Home Defence purposes. Even as matters stand,
however, at present, there is no reason why the services of
the National Volunteers should not be utilised for Defence
Work, such as that done by the National Volunteers some
time ago in Cork. I feel quite sure that, if a request were
made to the National Volunteers to undertake work of that kind,
they would be only too glad to agree. I would be glad to have
your view on this matter at your convenience. Very truly yours J. E. Redmond. Major—General Friend, C. B.
